title: Map and Compass Skills
summary: Using map and compass together for reliable navigation
body:
## Compass Anatomy

Before using a compass, understand its parts:

- **Baseplate** - The flat, transparent base. Often has a ruler for map measurements.
- **Direction-of-travel arrow** - Printed on the baseplate, pointing away from you. This is the direction you walk.
- **Rotating bezel (housing)** - The numbered ring (0-360 degrees) that you turn with your fingers.
- **Magnetic needle** - Floats freely inside the housing. The **red end points to magnetic north**.
- **Orienting arrow (gate)** - Printed on the floor of the housing. You rotate the bezel until the magnetic needle sits inside this arrow ("red in the shed").
- **Declination adjustment** - A small screw that offsets the orienting arrow to account for the difference between magnetic north and true north.

## Setting Declination

Magnetic north and true north (map north) differ by an amount called **declination**, which varies by location.

- Find the declination value on your map's legend or from a reliable source for your area
- If your compass has an adjustable declination screw, set it once and forget it
- If not, you must **add or subtract** declination from every bearing:
  - **East declination**: Subtract from your magnetic bearing to get a true bearing
  - **West declination**: Add to your magnetic bearing to get a true bearing
- Memory aid: **"East is least (subtract), West is best (add)"** when going from magnetic to true

**WARNING: Ignoring declination can put you off target by hundreds of meters over a few kilometers of travel.**

## Taking a Field Bearing

To determine the compass direction to a visible landmark:

1. Hold the compass flat at waist height with the direction-of-travel arrow pointing at your target
2. Rotate the bezel until the magnetic needle sits inside the orienting arrow ("red in the shed")
3. Read the number at the index line where the direction-of-travel arrow meets the bezel
4. That number is your bearing in degrees

## Following a Bearing

To walk on a specific bearing:

1. Set the desired bearing number at the index line by rotating the bezel
2. Hold the compass flat and turn your entire body until the needle sits in the gate
3. Look up along the direction-of-travel arrow and pick a **visible landmark** (a distinct tree, rock, or terrain feature)
4. Walk to that landmark (you can put the compass away while walking)
5. At the landmark, repeat the process: hold compass, align needle, pick new landmark

**Tip**: In low visibility, send a partner ahead and guide them onto the bearing line. This prevents veering.

## Back Bearing

A back bearing lets you retrace your path or verify your direction:

- Your back bearing is your forward bearing **plus or minus 180 degrees**
- If your forward bearing is 70 degrees, your back bearing is 250 degrees
- If your forward bearing is 220 degrees, your back bearing is 40 degrees
- To check: turn around and take a bearing back to where you came from. It should match your calculated back bearing.

## Triangulation (Finding Your Position)

If you can see 2-3 identifiable features that also appear on your map:

1. Take a compass bearing to the first feature
2. Convert to a back bearing (add or subtract 180 degrees)
3. On the map, place the compass edge on the feature with the back bearing set on the bezel
4. Rotate the entire compass on the map until the orienting lines in the housing are parallel to the map's north-south grid lines
5. Draw a line along the compass edge - you are somewhere on this line
6. Repeat for a second (and ideally third) feature
7. Where the lines intersect is your approximate position

Three lines will form a small triangle rather than a single point. Your position is inside this triangle. Smaller triangle means more accurate fix.

## Reading Contour Lines

Contour lines on a topographic map connect points of equal elevation:

- **Closely spaced lines** = steep terrain
- **Widely spaced lines** = gentle slope or flat ground
- **V-shapes pointing uphill** = valleys or drainages
- **V-shapes pointing downhill** = ridgelines or spurs
- **Closed circles** = hilltops or depressions (depressions have tick marks on the inner side)

## Estimating Distance on a Map

- Use the **scale bar** printed on the map margin
- Mark the distance between two points on the compass edge or a piece of paper
- Compare that length to the scale bar to read the real-world distance
- For winding routes, measure in short, straight segments and add them up
- **Rule of thumb**: On 1:25,000 maps, 4cm on the map equals 1km on the ground. On 1:50,000 maps, 2cm equals 1km.
